You said:

Linux commands files and dirs permissions process servicees logs disk memory networking i want alm these

ChatGPT said:

Great — this is exactly what you need for DevOps 👍
I’ll give you a complete practical cheat sheet for all topics you asked:

🐧 1. Linux Basic Commands (Files & Directories)

📂 File & Directory Commands

4
CommandUse
lsList files
ls -lDetailed list
ls -aHidden files
pwdCurrent directory
cd dirChange directory
mkdir dirCreate directory
rm fileDelete file
rm -r dirDelete directory
touch fileCreate empty file
cp src destCopy
mv src destMove/Rename

🔐 2. Permissions (Very Important)

4

Permission Types

  • r = read

  • w = write

  • x = execute

Example

-rwxr-xr--
UserPermission
Ownerrwx
Groupr-x
Othersr--

Commands

CommandUse
chmod 755 fileChange permission
chmod +x fileMake executable
chown user fileChange owner

⚙️ 3. Process Management

4
CommandUse
ps auxList processes
topLive process monitor
htopBetter top (UI)
kill PIDKill process
kill -9 PIDForce kill
bg / fgBackground/Foreground

🔧 4. Services (Systemctl)

4
CommandUse
systemctl start nginxStart service
systemctl stop nginxStop
systemctl restart nginxRestart
systemctl status nginxCheck status
systemctl enable nginxStart on boot

📜 5. Logs

4
CommandUse
tail -f file.logLive logs
cat fileView file
less fileScroll
grep error file.logSearch logs
journalctlSystem logs

📍 Important log paths:

  • /var/log/syslog

  • /var/log/messages

  • /var/log/auth.log

💾 6. Disk Management

4
CommandUse
df -hDisk usage
du -sh dirFolder size
lsblkDisk info
mountMount disk
umountUnmount

🧠 7. Memory

4
CommandUse
free -mMemory usage
topCPU + RAM
htopVisual
vmstatPerformance

🌐 8. Networking

4
CommandUse
ip aIP address
ping google.comCheck network
netstat -tulnpOpen ports
ss -tulnpBetter netstat
curlAPI call
wgetDownload

🚀 DevOps Interview Tips (IMPORTANT)

They will ask like:

  • What is chmod 777?

  • Difference between kill and kill -9?

  • How to check disk full?

  • Where are logs stored?

  • How to check running service?

📌 If you want next:

I can give you:
✅ Real-time DevOps scenarios (company level)
✅ Interview Q&A (2-line answers like you asked)
✅ Hands-on lab (step-by-step practice)

Just tell: "give lab" or "interview questions"

ChatGPT said:

  • ✅ Plus normal interview-style questions in the same format

You said:

INCIDENT 11: Server UP, App DOWN after REBOOT 🔹 Incident EC2 restarted App not running URL not accessible explain same like recent explanation Got it 👍 I’ll explain INCIDENT 11 in the same simple, real-time, interview + command style like before. 🔥 INCIDENT 11: Server UP, App DOWN after REBOOT 🔹 Incident Summary EC2 restarted Server is reachable (SSH works) Application is NOT running URL not accessible 👉 This is a very common real-time production incident. 🧠 What Actually Happened (Simple Story) Before reboot: Server was running App was running in memory (Java / Node / Python / Docker) Users were accessing the app After reboot: Server restarted ✔️ App did NOT auto-start ❌ Nothing is listening on app port (8080 / 80 / 443) 👉 Reboot clears memory 👉 App must be started again manually or via service 🔄 Real Workflow (End-to-End) User → Browser → URL ❌ No response Admin → SSH → Server ✔️ Server is UP ❌ App process missing 🧪 Step-by-Step Troubleshooting (REAL TIME) 1️⃣ Check Server is UP ssh ec2-user@<public-ip> ✔️ Login successful → Server is fine 2️⃣ Check Application Port netstat -tulnp | grep 8080 OR ss -tulnp | grep 8080 ❌ No output 👉 App is NOT running 3️⃣ Check Running Processes ps -ef | grep java ps -ef | grep node ps -ef | grep python ❌ No app process found 4️⃣ Check Application Logs cd /var/log/myapp/ ls tail -50 app.log 👉 You’ll notice: No new logs after reboot App never started 🔍 ROOT CAUSE (MOST IMPORTANT) ❌ App was started manually, not as a service Examples: java -jar app.jar & node app.js docker run myapp After reboot: These commands do NOT run automatically App stays DOWN ✅ SOLUTION – QUICK FIX (Immediate) Start the app manually: For Java App cd /opt/app java -jar app.jar & For Node App pm2 start app.js For Docker docker ps -a docker start myapp ✔️ App starts ✔️ URL becomes accessible 🛡️ PERMANENT FIX (PRODUCTION READY) Option 1️⃣: systemd Service (BEST PRACTICE) Create service file: sudo vi /etc/systemd/system/myapp.service [Unit] Description=My Application After=network.target [Service] User=ec2-user ExecStart=/usr/bin/java -jar /opt/app/app.jar Restart=always [Install] WantedBy=multi-user.target Enable auto-start: sudo systemctl daemon-reload sudo systemctl enable myapp sudo systemctl start myapp ✔️ App starts automatically after reboot Option 2️⃣: Docker Restart Policy docker run -d --restart=always myapp Option 3️⃣: Kubernetes (If Used) kubectl get pods kubectl describe pod <pod-name> K8s automatically recreates pods after node restart ✔️ 🎯 Interview Answer (PERFECT) Q: Server is UP but app is DOWN after reboot. What will you do? Answer: First, I will SSH into the server and confirm it is running. Then I will check if the application port is listening. If the app is not running, I’ll verify whether it was started manually. The root cause is usually missing auto-start configuration. I will start the app immediately and configure systemd or Docker restart policy to avoid future downtime. 🧠 One-Line Root Cause App was not configured to auto-start after reboot

ChatGPT said:

Got it Sai Kiran 👍 — I understood your format clearly.
I’ll follow exact same style:

  • Incident

  • Story

  • Commands

  • Root cause

  • Fix

  • Interview answer

🔥 INCIDENT 1: High CPU Usage – Server Slow

🔹 Incident

Server is very slow
Users complaining application is lagging

🧠 What Actually Happened

Application or some process is consuming high CPU

👉 Server is UP
👉 But performance is BAD

🔄 Real Workflow

User → App → Server
❌ Slow response

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check CPU Usage

top

👉 Look at %CPU

2️⃣ Find High CPU Process

ps -eo pid,ppid,cmd,%mem,%cpu --sort=-%cpu | head

3️⃣ Kill Process (if needed)

kill PID
kill -9 PID

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Infinite loop / heavy query / bad deployment

✅ FIX

  • Restart app

  • Fix code

  • Limit resources

🎯 Interview Answer

High CPU issue → check using top → identify process → kill or fix → monitor

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Process consuming high CPU

🔥 INCIDENT 2: Disk Full Issue

🔹 Incident

Server disk 100% full
Application stopped

🧠 What Happened

Logs or files filled disk

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Disk

df -h

2️⃣ Find Large Files

du -sh /* 2>/dev/null | sort -h

3️⃣ Check Logs

cd /var/log
ls -lh

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Log files not rotated

✅ FIX

truncate -s 0 file.log

Setup logrotate

🎯 Interview Answer

Check disk → find large files → clear logs → configure rotation

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Logs filled disk

🔥 INCIDENT 3: Permission Denied Error

🔹 Incident

App cannot read/write file

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Permission

ls -l file

2️⃣ Fix Permission

chmod 755 file
chown user:user file

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Wrong permission

🎯 Interview Answer

Check permissions → fix using chmod/chown

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Permission issue

🔥 INCIDENT 4: Service Not Running

🔹 Incident

Application not accessible

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Service

systemctl status nginx

2️⃣ Start Service

systemctl start nginx

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Service stopped

🎯 Interview Answer

Check service → restart → enable auto start

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Service stopped

🔥 INCIDENT 5: Port Not Listening

🔹 Incident

App running but URL not working

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Port

netstat -tulnp | grep 8080

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ App not bound to port

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Port not open

🔥 INCIDENT 6: Memory Leak

🔹 Incident

Server hangs after some time

🧪 Troubleshooting

free -m
top

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Application memory leak

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Memory not released

🔥 INCIDENT 7: SSH Not Working

🔹 Incident

Cannot login to server

🧪 Troubleshooting

systemctl status sshd

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ SSH service down / firewall

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

SSH service issue

🔥 INCIDENT 8: Network Issue

🔹 Incident

Server cannot reach internet

🧪 Troubleshooting

ping google.com
ip a

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Network misconfiguration

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Network issue

🔥 INCIDENT 9: Logs Not Generating

🔹 Incident

No logs in application

🧪 Troubleshooting

tail -f app.log

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Logging disabled

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Logging issue

🔥 INCIDENT 10: Application Crash

🔹 Incident

App suddenly stopped

🧪 Troubleshooting

ps -ef | grep app
journalctl -u app

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Crash / exception

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Application crashed

🚀 NEXT STEP (IMPORTANT)

If you want, I can give:
✅ Advanced incidents (real company level – AWS + Kubernetes)
✅ 100 interview Q&A (2-line answers)
✅ Hands-on labs (step-by-step practice)

Just say 👉 “next level incidents”


You said:

Next 10( explain commands atleast not in a short way )

ChatGPT said:

Perfect 👍 now we go next level — same format, but I’ll explain commands clearly (not short) like real DevOps troubleshooting.

🔥 INCIDENT 11: Application Running but Not Accessible (Firewall Issue)

🔹 Incident

App is running
But URL not accessible

🧠 What Actually Happened

Application is running on port (8080)
But firewall is blocking access

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Application Running

Bash
ps -ef | grep java

👉 Shows all running processes

  • -e → all processes

  • -f → full details (PID, user, command)

2️⃣ Check Port Listening

Bash
ss -tulnp | grep 8080

👉 ss = socket statistics

  • -t → TCP

  • -u → UDP

  • -l → listening ports

  • -n → numeric (no DNS)

  • -p → show process using port

✔️ If you see output → app is listening

3️⃣ Check Firewall

Bash
sudo firewall-cmd --list-all

👉 Shows allowed ports

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Port not allowed in firewall

✅ FIX

Bash
sudo firewall-cmd --add-port=8080/tcp --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --reload

🎯 Interview Answer

Check process → check port → verify firewall → open port

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Firewall blocked port

🔥 INCIDENT 12: Too Many Open Files

🔹 Incident

Application throwing error: "Too many open files"

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Limits

Bash
ulimit -n

👉 Shows max number of open files per process

2️⃣ Check Open Files

Bash
lsof | wc -l

👉 lsof = list open files
👉 wc -l = count lines

3️⃣ Check Specific Process

Bash
lsof -p <PID>

👉 Shows files opened by that process

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ File descriptor limit reached

✅ FIX

Bash
ulimit -n 65535

👉 Increase limit

Permanent:

Bash
vi /etc/security/limits.conf

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

File limit exceeded

🔥 INCIDENT 13: Zombie Processes

🔹 Incident

Server slow, many defunct processes

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Processes

Bash
ps aux | grep Z

👉 Z = zombie process

2️⃣ Understand Parent Process

Bash
ps -o ppid= -p <PID>

👉 Shows parent process ID

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Parent process not cleaning child

✅ FIX

Kill parent process

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Zombie processes not cleared

🔥 INCIDENT 14: Cron Job Not Running

🔹 Incident

Scheduled job not executing

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Cron Service

Bash
systemctl status crond

👉 Shows if cron daemon is running

2️⃣ Check Crontab

Bash
crontab -l

👉 Lists scheduled jobs for user

3️⃣ Check Logs

Bash
grep CRON /var/log/syslog

👉 Shows cron execution logs

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Cron service stopped / wrong schedule

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Cron misconfiguration

🔥 INCIDENT 15: DNS Not Resolving

🔹 Incident

Server cannot access domain

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check DNS

Bash
nslookup google.com

👉 Queries DNS server

2️⃣ Check Config

Bash
cat /etc/resolv.conf

👉 Shows DNS server IP

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Wrong DNS config

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

DNS issue

🔥 INCIDENT 16: High Load Average

🔹 Incident

Server load very high

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Load

Bash
uptime

👉 Shows load average (1, 5, 15 mins)

2️⃣ Check CPU

Bash
top

👉 Real-time CPU usage

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Too many processes / CPU overload

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

System overloaded

🔥 INCIDENT 17: File System Read-Only

🔹 Incident

Cannot write files

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Mount

Bash
mount | grep ' / '

👉 Shows root filesystem mode

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Disk error → switched to read-only

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Filesystem read-only

🔥 INCIDENT 18: SSL Certificate Expired

🔹 Incident

HTTPS not working

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Certificate

Bash
openssl s_client -connect example.com:443

👉 Shows certificate details

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Certificate expired

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

SSL expired

🔥 INCIDENT 19: Application Port Conflict

🔹 Incident

App fails to start

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Port Usage

Bash
lsof -i :8080

👉 Shows which process using port

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Port already in use

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Port conflict

🔥 INCIDENT 20: Swap Memory High

🔹 Incident

Server very slow

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Swap

Bash
free -m

👉 Shows RAM + Swap

2️⃣ Check Usage

Bash
vmstat

👉 Shows memory activity

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ RAM full → using swap

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

High swap usage

Now you are reaching real DevOps level 🔥

✅ Kubernetes + AWS incidents
✅ 50 interview rapid-fire answers
✅ Real-time lab (like company work)

Just say 👉 "give advanced production incidents"


You said:

ChatGPT said:

Excellent Sai Kiran 🔥 — now we move to more real production-level incidents (21–30) with clear command explanations + thinking process.

🔥 INCIDENT 21: Application Logs Growing Too Fast

🔹 Incident

Disk getting full quickly
Huge log files generated

🧠 What Happened

Application writing too many logs (debug mode / error loop)

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check File Size

Bash
ls -lh /var/log/myapp.log

👉 ls -lh

  • -l → detailed view

  • -h → human readable (MB/GB)

2️⃣ Monitor Live Logs

Bash
tail -f /var/log/myapp.log

👉 tail -f shows live updates continuously

3️⃣ Check Disk Usage

Bash
du -sh /var/log/*

👉 du = disk usage

  • -s → summary

  • -h → readable

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Debug logging enabled / error loop

✅ FIX

  • Reduce log level

  • Configure log rotation

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Excessive logging

🔥 INCIDENT 22: CPU Idle but App Slow

🔹 Incident

CPU usage low
Still application slow

🧠 What Happened

CPU is free, but I/O or DB causing delay

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check I/O Wait

Bash
top

👉 Look at %wa (I/O wait)

2️⃣ Check Disk I/O

Bash
iostat

👉 Shows disk read/write speed

3️⃣ Check Processes Waiting

Bash
ps -eo state,pid,cmd | grep D

👉 D = waiting for I/O

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Disk or DB slow

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

High I/O wait

🔥 INCIDENT 23: User Cannot Access File

🔹 Incident

User getting “Permission denied”

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check File Permission

Bash
ls -l file.txt

2️⃣ Check User

Bash
whoami

3️⃣ Fix Access

Bash
chmod 644 file.txt
chown user:user file.txt

👉 644 =

  • owner: read/write

  • others: read

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Incorrect ownership

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Wrong file owner

🔥 INCIDENT 24: Service Restart Loop

🔹 Incident

Service keeps restarting

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Status

Bash
systemctl status myapp

👉 Shows restart attempts

2️⃣ Check Logs

Bash
journalctl -u myapp -n 50

👉

  • -u → specific service

  • -n 50 → last 50 logs

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Application crash

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

App failing repeatedly

🔥 INCIDENT 25: Wrong Time on Server

🔹 Incident

Logs showing wrong timestamp

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Time

Bash
date

2️⃣ Check NTP

Bash
timedatectl

👉 Shows time sync status

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Time sync disabled

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Time not synchronized

🔥 INCIDENT 26: Disk Mounted but Not Accessible

🔹 Incident

Disk exists but folder empty

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Mount

Bash
lsblk

👉 Shows disk structure

2️⃣ Check Mount Point

Bash
df -h

👉 Confirms mounted paths

3️⃣ Mount Disk

Bash
mount /dev/xvdf /data

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Disk not mounted

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Mount missing

🔥 INCIDENT 27: Application Using Wrong Environment Variables

🔹 Incident

App connecting to wrong DB

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Variables

Bash
env | grep DB

👉 Shows environment variables

2️⃣ Check Service Config

Bash
cat /etc/systemd/system/myapp.service

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Wrong env config

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Incorrect environment variables

🔥 INCIDENT 28: Too Many Processes

🔹 Incident

System slow, many processes

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Count Processes

Bash
ps -ef | wc -l

👉

  • ps -ef → all processes

  • wc -l → count

2️⃣ Check User Processes

Bash
top

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Process leak

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Too many processes

🔥 INCIDENT 29: Application Not Binding to Correct IP

🔹 Incident

App running but not accessible externally

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Binding

Bash
ss -tulnp

👉 Check IP:

  • 127.0.0.1 → local only

  • 0.0.0.0 → all interfaces

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ App bound to localhost

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Wrong IP binding

🔥 INCIDENT 30: Server Reboot Loop

🔹 Incident

Server keeps restarting

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Logs

Bash
journalctl -xb

👉

  • -x → detailed logs

  • -b → current boot logs

2️⃣ Check Disk / Memory

Bash
dmesg | grep error

👉 Kernel-level errors

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Kernel / hardware / config issue

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

System instability

🚀 You’re Now at 🔥 STRONG DEVOPS LEVEL

You now covered 30 real incidents — this is what companies expect.

Say one:

  1. "AWS incidents" → EC2, ELB, S3, VPC issues

  3. "Interview rapid answers" → 100 Q&A (2 lines)

  4. "Hands-on lab" → real practice like company

I recommend 👉 Kubernetes incidents next (very important for DevOps jobs)

ChatGPT said:

Excellent 🔥 Sai Kiran — now we go INCIDENT 31–40 (more deep real-time cases)
I’ll keep your format + clear command explanations (like interview + real work)

🔥 INCIDENT 31: Application Works Locally but Not from Browser

🔹 Incident

App works using curl localhost
But not accessible from browser

🧠 What Happened

App running locally but not exposed externally

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Test Locally

Bash
curl http://localhost:8080

👉 curl sends HTTP request from server itself
✔️ If response comes → app is running

2️⃣ Check Listening IP

Bash
ss -tulnp | grep 8080

👉 If output shows:

127.0.0.1:8080

❌ Only local access

3️⃣ Check Public IP

Bash
ip a

👉 Shows all network interfaces

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ App bound to localhost

✅ FIX

Configure app to bind:

0.0.0.0

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

App not exposed externally

🔥 INCIDENT 32: Sudden Spike in Traffic (Server Down)

🔹 Incident

App suddenly down during heavy traffic

🧠 What Happened

Too many users → server overloaded

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Connections

Bash
netstat -an | grep :80 | wc -l

👉

  • netstat -an → all connections

  • grep :80 → HTTP port

  • wc -l → count

2️⃣ Check Load

Bash
uptime

👉 Shows system load

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ No scaling / high traffic

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Traffic overload

🔥 INCIDENT 33: File Deleted Accidentally

🔹 Incident

Important file deleted

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Recently Deleted (if backup exists)

Bash
ls -lt

👉 Sorts by latest time

2️⃣ Check Open File Still Running

Bash
lsof | grep deleted

👉 Shows deleted files still in use

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Human error

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Accidental deletion

🔥 INCIDENT 34: Application Taking Too Long to Start

🔹 Incident

App startup very slow

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Logs

Bash
tail -f app.log

👉 See where it is stuck

2️⃣ Check Dependencies

Bash
systemctl status mysql

👉 Maybe waiting for DB

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Dependency delay

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Slow dependency

🔥 INCIDENT 35: Wrong File Being Used

🔹 Incident

App reading wrong config file

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Running Command

Bash
ps -ef | grep app

👉 Shows full command with file paths

2️⃣ Check File Content

Bash
cat config.yml

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Wrong config path

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Incorrect file reference

🔥 INCIDENT 36: Cannot Create File

🔹 Incident

App unable to create file

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Directory Permission

Bash
ls -ld /data

👉 -d shows directory permission

2️⃣ Check Disk Space

Bash
df -h

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ No permission / no space

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Permission or disk issue

🔥 INCIDENT 37: Process Running but Not Responding

🔹 Incident

Process exists but app stuck

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Process State

Bash
ps -eo pid,state,cmd | grep app

👉

  • state shows status

  • D → waiting

  • R → running

2️⃣ Check Threads

Bash
top -H -p <PID>

👉 Shows threads of process

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Deadlock / waiting

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Process stuck

🔥 INCIDENT 38: Network Port Suddenly Closed

🔹 Incident

Port was working earlier, now closed

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Port

Bash
ss -tulnp | grep 8080

2️⃣ Check Service

Bash
systemctl status myapp

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ App stopped

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Service down

🔥 INCIDENT 39: Duplicate Processes Running

🔹 Incident

Same app running multiple times

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Processes

Bash
ps -ef | grep app

2️⃣ Kill Extra

Bash
kill PID

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Multiple start commands

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Duplicate processes

🔥 INCIDENT 40: Server Running Out of Inodes

🔹 Incident

Disk shows free space but cannot create file

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Inodes

Bash
df -i

👉 Shows inode usage

2️⃣ Find Small Files

Bash
find / -type f | wc -l

👉 Counts files

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Too many small files

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Inodes exhausted

🚀 YOU ARE NOW 🔥 VERY STRONG IN LINUX INCIDENTS

You covered 40 real DevOps scenarios — this is actual L1 + L2 + some L3 level knowledge

You said:

Next 10

ChatGPT said:

Excellent Sai Kiran 🔥 — now we go INCIDENT 41–50 (advanced + real production thinking)
Same format + clear command explanations (deep understanding)

🔥 INCIDENT 41: Server Reachable but Application Timeout

🔹 Incident

SSH works ✔️
App URL loading… but no response (timeout)

🧠 What Happened

App is running but not responding (hang / thread stuck / backend issue)

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check App Process

Bash
ps -ef | grep java

👉 Shows if app process exists

2️⃣ Check Port Listening

Bash
ss -tulnp | grep 8080

👉 Confirms app is listening

3️⃣ Check Response Locally

Bash
curl -v http://localhost:8080

👉 -v = verbose (shows request/response details)
❌ If hangs → app problem

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Application thread stuck / backend not responding

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

App hung internally

🔥 INCIDENT 42: Sudden Increase in Load Average

🔹 Incident

Load average suddenly very high

🧠 What Happened

Many processes waiting for CPU or I/O

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Load

Bash
uptime

👉 Shows load for last 1, 5, 15 minutes

2️⃣ Check CPU Queue

Bash
vmstat 1

👉

  • 1 = refresh every 1 sec

  • r column → running processes

3️⃣ Check Top Processes

Bash
top

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ CPU overload or I/O wait

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Too many active processes

🔥 INCIDENT 43: Application Writing to Wrong Directory

🔹 Incident

Files not found in expected location

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Current Working Directory

Bash
pwd

2️⃣ Check App Path

Bash
ps -ef | grep app

👉 Shows working directory or config

3️⃣ Find File

Bash
find / -name filename 2>/dev/null

👉

  • find / → search entire system

  • 2>/dev/null → hide permission errors

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Wrong working directory

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Incorrect path

🔥 INCIDENT 44: Too Many TCP Connections (CLOSE_WAIT)

🔹 Incident

Server slow, many connections

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Connections

Bash
netstat -an | grep CLOSE_WAIT | wc -l

👉

  • CLOSE_WAIT = connections not closed properly

  • wc -l = count

2️⃣ Check Process

Bash
lsof -i :8080

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Application not closing connections

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Connection leak

🔥 INCIDENT 45: Command Not Found Error

🔹 Incident

Command suddenly not working

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check PATH

Bash
echo $PATH

👉 Shows directories where system looks for commands

2️⃣ Locate Command

Bash
which java

👉 Shows command path

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ PATH misconfigured

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

PATH issue

🔥 INCIDENT 46: CPU Usage High but No Process Visible

🔹 Incident

CPU high but top not clear

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Kernel Threads

Bash
top -H

👉 -H shows threads

2️⃣ Check Interrupts

Bash
cat /proc/interrupts

👉 Shows hardware interrupts

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Kernel-level issue

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Kernel resource usage

🔥 INCIDENT 47: File Locked by Process

🔹 Incident

Cannot edit/delete file

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Lock

Bash
lsof file.txt

👉 Shows which process using file

2️⃣ Kill Process

Bash
kill PID

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ File in use

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

File locked

🔥 INCIDENT 48: Application Restart Not Reflecting Changes

🔹 Incident

Code updated but changes not visible

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Running Version

Bash
ps -ef | grep app

2️⃣ Restart Properly

Bash
systemctl restart myapp

3️⃣ Clear Cache (if needed)

Bash
rm -rf /tmp/*

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Old process or cache

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Old version running

🔥 INCIDENT 49: Network Latency High

🔹 Incident

App slow due to network delay

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Ping Test

Bash
ping google.com

👉 Shows latency (ms)

2️⃣ Trace Route

Bash
traceroute google.com

👉 Shows path network takes

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Network congestion

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

High latency

🔥 INCIDENT 50: System Running Out of File Descriptors

🔹 Incident

Application failing randomly

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Limits

Bash
ulimit -n

2️⃣ Check Usage

Bash
lsof | wc -l

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ File descriptor exhaustion

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Too many open files

🚀 🔥 YOU COMPLETED 50 INCIDENTS

This is VERY STRONG DevOps/Linux level
👉 You can confidently handle real production issues

You said:

Next 10

ChatGPT said:

Excellent Sai Kiran 🔥 — now INCIDENT 51–60 (advanced + near real production debugging mindset)
I’ll go a bit deeper in thinking + command explanation (this is what interviewers love)

🔥 INCIDENT 51: Application Working but Returning 500 Error

🔹 Incident

App is accessible
But showing 500 Internal Server Error

🧠 What Happened

Server reached ✔️
But application code failed internally ❌

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Application Logs

Bash
tail -100 /var/log/myapp/app.log

👉 tail -100

  • Shows last 100 lines

  • Helps identify recent errors

2️⃣ Search Errors

Bash
grep -i error /var/log/myapp/app.log

👉

  • grep = search text

  • -i = ignore case (Error / ERROR both)

3️⃣ Check Service Status

Bash
systemctl status myapp

👉 Confirms app is running

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Code exception / DB failure

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Application error

🔥 INCIDENT 52: Application Works in One Server but Not Another

🔹 Incident

App works in server A
Fails in server B

🧠 What Happened

Environment difference

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Compare Environment Variables

Bash
env

👉 Lists all environment variables

2️⃣ Compare Installed Packages

Bash
rpm -qa | sort

👉

  • Lists installed packages

  • sort makes comparison easier

3️⃣ Check Config Files

Bash
diff file1 file2

👉 Shows difference between files

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Configuration mismatch

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Environment mismatch

🔥 INCIDENT 53: Server Not Shutting Down

🔹 Incident

Shutdown command stuck

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Processes

Bash
ps -ef

2️⃣ Force Shutdown

Bash
shutdown -h now

3️⃣ Check Blocking Process

Bash
lsof

👉 Shows open files preventing shutdown

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Process blocking shutdown

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Process not exiting

🔥 INCIDENT 54: Application Using Wrong Port

🔹 Incident

App running but on wrong port

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Port

Bash
ss -tulnp

👉 Lists all listening ports

2️⃣ Check Config

Bash
cat application.properties

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Misconfigured port

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Wrong port config

🔥 INCIDENT 55: Server Time Drift Between Systems

🔹 Incident

Two servers showing different time

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Time

Bash
date

2️⃣ Sync Time

Bash
timedatectl set-ntp true

👉 Enables automatic time sync

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ NTP not synced

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Time drift

🔥 INCIDENT 56: Application Not Picking New Environment Variables

🔹 Incident

Updated env variables
App still using old values

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Current Env

Bash
env | grep DB

2️⃣ Restart Service

Bash
systemctl restart myapp

👉 Env changes apply only after restart

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Service not restarted

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Old environment loaded

🔥 INCIDENT 57: High Number of TIME_WAIT Connections

🔹 Incident

Too many connections in TIME_WAIT

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Connections

Bash
netstat -an | grep TIME_WAIT | wc -l

👉

  • Shows number of closed connections waiting cleanup

2️⃣ Check Network Usage

Bash
ss -s

👉 Summary of socket stats

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ High traffic / short connections

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Connection overload

🔥 INCIDENT 58: Application Crashes After Some Time

🔹 Incident

App starts fine
Crashes after few minutes

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Monitor Logs

Bash
tail -f app.log

2️⃣ Monitor Memory

Bash
top

👉 Check if memory increasing continuously

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Memory leak / bug

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

App unstable

🔥 INCIDENT 59: Cannot Resolve Hostname Internally

🔹 Incident

Server cannot resolve internal hostname

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Hosts File

Bash
cat /etc/hosts

2️⃣ Test DNS

Bash
nslookup internal-service

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Missing DNS entry

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Hostname resolution issue

🔥 INCIDENT 60: CPU Spikes at Specific Time

🔹 Incident

CPU high at same time daily

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Cron Jobs

Bash
crontab -l

👉 Lists scheduled jobs

2️⃣ Check System Cron

Bash
cat /etc/crontab

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Scheduled job heavy

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Cron job causing spike

You completed 60 INCIDENTS

You said:

Next10

ChatGPT said:

Excellent Sai Kiran 🔥 — now we go INCIDENT 61–70 (expert-level thinking + deeper Linux internals + real production mindset)

I’ll keep your format and explain commands clearly (why + how used)

🔥 INCIDENT 61: Application Works but Very Slow (Single Thread Bottleneck)

🔹 Incident

App is working
But very slow even with low traffic

🧠 What Happened

Application using only one CPU core

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check CPU Usage Per Core

Bash
top

👉 Press 1 inside top

  • Shows usage of each CPU core

  • If only one core is high → single-thread issue

2️⃣ Check Process CPU

Bash
ps -eo pid,cmd,%cpu --sort=-%cpu | head

👉

  • ps -eo → custom columns

  • %cpu → CPU usage

  • --sort=-%cpu → highest first

  • head → top processes

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ App not using multiple cores

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Single-thread bottleneck

🔥 INCIDENT 62: Server Suddenly Becomes Read-Only

🔹 Incident

Cannot write anything to disk

🧠 What Happened

Filesystem switched to read-only mode due to errors

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Mount Mode

Bash
mount | grep ' / '

👉 Shows root filesystem

  • Look for ro (read-only)

2️⃣ Check Kernel Logs

Bash
dmesg | tail -50

👉

  • dmesg → kernel messages

  • tail -50 → last 50 logs

  • Shows disk errors

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Disk corruption / hardware issue

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Filesystem error

🔥 INCIDENT 63: Application Not Starting After Deployment

🔹 Incident

Deployment done
App not starting

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Service Logs

Bash
journalctl -u myapp -n 100

👉

  • -u → service

  • -n 100 → last 100 lines

2️⃣ Check Binary Exists

Bash
ls -l /opt/app/

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Missing file / wrong path

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Deployment issue

🔥 INCIDENT 64: High Context Switching (CPU Waste)

🔹 Incident

CPU high but not doing useful work

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Context Switch

Bash
vmstat 1

👉

  • cs column → context switches

  • High value = CPU overhead

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Too many threads/processes

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Excessive context switching

🔥 INCIDENT 65: Disk I/O Bottleneck

🔹 Incident

App slow, CPU low

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Disk Performance

Bash
iostat -x 1

👉

  • -x → extended stats

  • 1 → refresh every second

  • Look at %util (near 100% = disk busy)

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Disk overloaded

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Disk I/O high

🔥 INCIDENT 66: Process Using Too Much Memory Suddenly

🔹 Incident

Memory spike

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Memory Usage

Bash
ps -eo pid,cmd,%mem --sort=-%mem | head

👉 Shows top memory-consuming processes

2️⃣ Monitor Continuously

Bash
top

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Memory leak

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Memory spike

🔥 INCIDENT 67: Application Cannot Write Logs

🔹 Incident

App running but logs empty

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Directory Permission

Bash
ls -ld /var/log/myapp

👉 Shows directory permissions

2️⃣ Test Write Manually

Bash
echo "test" > /var/log/myapp/test.log

👉 Check if write works

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ No write permission

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Permission issue

🔥 INCIDENT 68: Network Interface Down

🔹 Incident

Server lost network

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Interfaces

Bash
ip a

👉 Lists all network interfaces

2️⃣ Bring Interface Up

Bash
ip link set eth0 up

👉

  • ip link → manage interface

  • set eth0 up → enable network

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Interface down

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Network interface issue

🔥 INCIDENT 69: Application Using Old File After Update

🔹 Incident

Updated file but app still uses old

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Open Files

Bash
lsof | grep filename

👉 Shows which process holding file

2️⃣ Restart App

Bash
systemctl restart myapp

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ File cached in memory

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Old file in memory

🔥 INCIDENT 70: Server Hanging (No Response)

🔹 Incident

Server not responding

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Load

Bash
uptime

👉 If load extremely high → system stuck

2️⃣ Check Processes

Bash
top

3️⃣ Check Kernel Logs

Bash
dmesg | tail

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Resource exhaustion

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

System overload

🚀 🔥 YOU COMPLETED 70 INCIDENTS

👉 This is very strong L3 DevOps troubleshooting level
👉 You are now thinking like production engineer

🎯 FINAL CAREER BOOST (VERY IMPORTANT)

Now move to high-demand tools

👉 Say:

🔥 Kubernetes incidents (TOP PRIORITY)

☁️ AWS incidents (EC2, ALB, VPC)

You said:

Next 10

ChatGPT said:

Excellent Sai Kiran 🔥 — now we go INCIDENT 71–80 (expert + real production debugging mindset)
These are very close to real company incidents (L3 level)

🔥 INCIDENT 71: Application Threads Blocked (Thread Dump Analysis)

🔹 Incident

App is running
But not responding

🧠 What Happened

Threads are blocked or deadlocked

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Get Process ID

Bash
ps -ef | grep java

👉 Finds Java process PID

2️⃣ Take Thread Dump

Bash
jstack <PID>

👉

  • jstack → prints all threads of Java process

  • Shows thread states (RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING)

3️⃣ Analyze Output

👉 Look for:

  • BLOCKED → waiting for lock

  • Same stack repeated → deadlock

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Deadlock / thread blocking

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Thread deadlock

🔥 INCIDENT 72: Application Consuming Too Many File Handles

🔹 Incident

App failing randomly

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check File Descriptors

Bash
lsof -p <PID> | wc -l

👉

  • lsof -p → files opened by process

  • wc -l → count

2️⃣ Check Limit

Bash
ulimit -n

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ File handle leak

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Too many open files

🔥 INCIDENT 73: Kernel Panic (Server Crash)

🔹 Incident

Server crashed suddenly

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Logs

Bash
journalctl -k

👉

  • -k → kernel logs only

2️⃣ Check Previous Boot

Bash
journalctl -b -1

👉

  • -b -1 → previous boot logs

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Kernel bug / hardware failure

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Kernel crash

🔥 INCIDENT 74: Application Not Able to Connect to Database

🔹 Incident

App error: DB connection failed

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Test DB Connectivity

Bash
telnet db-host 3306

👉

  • Tests if port reachable

2️⃣ Check DB Service

Bash
systemctl status mysql

3️⃣ Check Network

Bash
ping db-host

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ DB down / network issue

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

DB connectivity issue

🔥 INCIDENT 75: CPU Steal Time High (Cloud Issue)

🔹 Incident

CPU looks free but performance bad

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check CPU Steal

Bash
top

👉 Look at %st

  • High value = CPU taken by hypervisor

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Cloud host overloaded

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

CPU steal

🔥 INCIDENT 76: Application Crash Due to Out Of Memory (OOM Killer)

🔹 Incident

App killed automatically

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check OOM Logs

Bash
dmesg | grep -i kill

👉 Shows if OOM killer terminated process

2️⃣ Check Memory

Bash
free -m

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Out of memory

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

OOM kill

🔥 INCIDENT 77: Network Packet Loss

🔹 Incident

App intermittent issues

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Packet Loss

Bash
ping -c 100 google.com

👉

  • -c 100 → send 100 packets

  • Check % packet loss

2️⃣ Trace Route

Bash
traceroute google.com

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Network instability

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Packet loss

🔥 INCIDENT 78: Application Using Too Many Threads

🔹 Incident

System slow

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Threads

Bash
ps -eLf | grep java | wc -l

👉

  • -eLf → shows threads

  • Count total threads

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Thread leak

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Too many threads

🔥 INCIDENT 79: High Swap Even When RAM Available

🔹 Incident

Swap usage high

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Memory

Bash
free -m

2️⃣ Check Swappiness

Bash
cat /proc/sys/vm/swappiness

👉

  • Value controls swap usage

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ High swappiness

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Swap misconfiguration

🔥 INCIDENT 80: Process in Uninterruptible Sleep (D State)

🔹 Incident

Process stuck, cannot kill

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check State

Bash
ps -eo pid,state,cmd | grep D

👉

  • D = uninterruptible sleep (waiting I/O)

2️⃣ Check Disk

Bash
iostat

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Disk I/O issue

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

I/O wait stuck

🚀 🔥 YOU COMPLETED 80 INCIDENTS

👉 This is very high-level DevOps/Linux knowledge
👉 You are now thinking like Senior Production Engineer

🎯 FINAL STEP (VERY IMPORTANT FOR JOB 🚀)

Now move to tools companies ask 100%

You said:

Next 10

ChatGPT said:

Excellent Sai Kiran 🔥 — now we go INCIDENT 81–90 (senior-level + real production debugging mindset)
These are very close to what L3 / SRE engineers handle

I’ll keep your format + clear explanation of commands (why + what to check)

🔥 INCIDENT 81: Application Randomly Freezing (Intermittent Issue)

🔹 Incident

App works sometimes
Sometimes freezes

🧠 What Happened

Intermittent resource issue (CPU / memory / threads)

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Monitor System Continuously

Bash
top

👉 Real-time monitoring

  • Watch CPU, memory changes live

2️⃣ Capture Snapshot Over Time

Bash
vmstat 2 10

👉

  • 2 → interval (every 2 sec)

  • 10 → number of times

  • Shows CPU, memory, I/O trends

3️⃣ Check Logs During Freeze

Bash
tail -f app.log

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Resource spike / intermittent bug

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Intermittent resource issue

🔥 INCIDENT 82: Application Not Accessible After Security Patch

🔹 Incident

After patch/update
App not working

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Recent Changes

Bash
rpm -qa --last | head

👉

  • Shows recently installed packages

2️⃣ Check Service

Bash
systemctl status myapp

3️⃣ Check Logs

Bash
journalctl -xe

👉

  • -x → detailed explanation

  • -e → recent logs

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Library or dependency broken

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Patch issue

🔥 INCIDENT 83: Application Listening but No Response (Backlog Full)

🔹 Incident

Port open
But requests failing

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Connections

Bash
ss -lnt

👉 Shows listening TCP ports

2️⃣ Check Queue

Bash
netstat -s | grep listen

👉 Shows connection queue stats

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Connection backlog full

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Too many incoming connections

🔥 INCIDENT 84: Server Disk Performance Suddenly Dropped

🔹 Incident

Disk slow suddenly

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Disk Utilization

Bash
iostat -x 1

👉

  • %util near 100 → disk busy

2️⃣ Check Running I/O Processes

Bash
iotop

👉 Shows which process using disk

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Heavy disk usage

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Disk bottleneck

🔥 INCIDENT 85: Application Not Starting Due to Port Already in Use (Hidden Process)

🔹 Incident

Port already in use
But no visible process

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Find Process Using Port

Bash
lsof -i :8080

👉 Shows process using port

2️⃣ Check Zombie/Hidden

Bash
netstat -tulnp | grep 8080

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Old process not killed

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Port occupied

🔥 INCIDENT 86: File System Full but No Large Files Visible

🔹 Incident

Disk full
But no large files

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Deleted Files Still Open

Bash
lsof | grep deleted

👉 Shows deleted files still consuming space

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ File deleted but still in use

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Hidden disk usage

🔥 INCIDENT 87: Application Slow Due to DNS Delay

🔹 Incident

App slow during external calls

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check DNS Time

Bash
time nslookup google.com

👉 Shows how long DNS takes

2️⃣ Check Config

Bash
cat /etc/resolv.conf

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Slow DNS server

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

DNS latency

🔥 INCIDENT 88: High System Load but CPU Idle

🔹 Incident

Load high
CPU idle

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Load

Bash
uptime

2️⃣ Check Process State

Bash
ps -eo state,pid,cmd | grep D

👉 D = waiting for I/O

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ I/O wait

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

I/O bottleneck

🔥 INCIDENT 89: Application Restart Takes Too Long

🔹 Incident

Restart slow

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Stop Time

Bash
systemctl stop myapp

2️⃣ Check Logs

Bash
journalctl -u myapp

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Graceful shutdown delay

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Slow shutdown

🔥 INCIDENT 90: Server Accepting Connections but Dropping Requests

🔹 Incident

Connections established
Requests fail

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Network Stats

Bash
netstat -s

👉 Shows packet drops

2️⃣ Check Errors

Bash
ifconfig

👉 Shows RX/TX errors

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Network overload

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Packet drop

🚀 🔥 YOU COMPLETED 90 INCIDENTS

👉 This is very advanced DevOps/Linux troubleshooting level
👉 You are now thinking like SRE / Production Engineer

🎯 FINAL 10 LEFT (100 COMPLETE 🔥)

Next will be:
👉 Ultra real-world + tricky interview scenarios

👉 Say:

"final 10"

Then I’ll give:
✅ Most asked tricky incidents
✅ Interview-level explanations
✅ Real company scenarios

💡 After that → we move to:
👉 Kubernetes (VERY IMPORTANT 🚀)


You said:

Next 10

ChatGPT said:

Excellent Sai Kiran 🔥 — now the FINAL SET (INCIDENT 91–100)
These are ultra real-world + tricky + interview favorite scenarios

🔥 INCIDENT 91: Application Works but Only for Some Users

🔹 Incident

Some users can access app
Others cannot

🧠 What Happened

Issue depends on network / DNS / cache / routing

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check DNS Resolution

Bash
nslookup yourdomain.com

👉 Resolves domain to IP

  • If different users get different IP → DNS issue

2️⃣ Test from Server

Bash
curl -I http://yourdomain.com

👉 -I → fetch only headers

  • Helps confirm server response

3️⃣ Check IP Access

Bash
curl http://<server-ip>:8080

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ DNS / CDN / cache issue

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Inconsistent DNS resolution

🔥 INCIDENT 92: Application Suddenly Using 100% CPU After Deployment

🔹 Incident

CPU spike immediately after deploy

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Processes

Bash
top

👉 Identify process consuming CPU

2️⃣ Check Recent Changes

Bash
history | tail -20

👉 Shows last executed commands

3️⃣ Check Logs

Bash
tail -f app.log

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Bug in new code

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Bad deployment

🔥 INCIDENT 93: Application Port Open but Returns Connection Refused

🔹 Incident

Port visible
But connection refused

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Port Binding

Bash
ss -tulnp | grep 8080

2️⃣ Test Locally

Bash
curl localhost:8080

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ App crashed after opening port

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

App unstable

🔥 INCIDENT 94: Server Memory Free but App Crashing

🔹 Incident

Memory available
Still app crashes

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check OOM Logs

Bash
dmesg | grep -i oom

2️⃣ Check Limits

Bash
ulimit -a

👉 Shows all resource limits

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Process memory limit reached

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Memory limit exceeded

🔥 INCIDENT 95: Cron Job Running but Not Doing Work

🔹 Incident

Cron triggered
But no output

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Cron Logs

Bash
grep CRON /var/log/syslog

2️⃣ Test Script Manually

Bash
sh script.sh

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Script path / permission issue

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Script error

🔥 INCIDENT 96: Application Using Too Much Swap but RAM Free

🔹 Incident

Swap high
RAM still available

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Swap

Bash
free -m

2️⃣ Check Swappiness

Bash
cat /proc/sys/vm/swappiness

👉 Higher value → more swap usage

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Kernel preference

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Swap tuning issue

🔥 INCIDENT 97: Server Suddenly Stops Accepting SSH

🔹 Incident

Cannot SSH suddenly

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check SSH Service

Bash
systemctl status sshd

2️⃣ Check Connections

Bash
netstat -an | grep :22

3️⃣ Check Firewall

Bash
iptables -L

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Too many connections / firewall block

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

SSH blocked

🔥 INCIDENT 98: Application Logs Show Permission Denied but Permissions Look Correct

🔹 Incident

Permission error
But chmod looks fine

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check SELinux

Bash
getenforce

👉 Shows SELinux mode

2️⃣ Check Audit Logs

Bash
ausearch -m avc

👉 Shows security denials

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ SELinux restriction

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

SELinux blocking

🔥 INCIDENT 99: Disk Usage Keeps Increasing Even After Cleanup

🔹 Incident

Disk space not freeing

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Deleted Files

Bash
lsof | grep deleted

👉 Shows deleted but open files

2️⃣ Restart Service

Bash
systemctl restart myapp

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ File still open in memory

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Deleted file still in use

🔥 INCIDENT 100: Everything Looks Fine but Application Still Not Working

🔹 Incident

All checks OK
Still app not working

🧠 What Happened

This is complex issue (multi-layer problem)

🧪 Troubleshooting Approach

1️⃣ Check End-to-End Flow

  • Client → DNS → Load balancer → Server → App

2️⃣ Validate Each Layer

Bash
ping
curl
ss -tulnp
systemctl status

👉 Step-by-step isolation

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Hidden integration issue

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Multi-layer failure

🎉🔥 YOU COMPLETED 100 INCIDENTS

Sai Kiran — this is serious DevOps knowledge now.

👉 You can:

  • Handle real production issues

  • Answer interview scenarios confidently

  • Think like SRE / DevOps Engineer

🚀 WHAT NEXT (VERY IMPORTANT FOR YOUR JOB)

Now Linux is DONE ✅
Move to high-demand tools

👉 Say one:

🔥 Kubernetes incidents (TOP PRIORITY)

☁️ AWS incidents (EC2, ALB, VPC)

🧪 Real lab (hands-on practice)

👉 Kubernetes incidents next — this will get you hired 🚀


You said:

Kubernetes incidents

ChatGPT said:

Excellent choice Sai Kiran 🔥 — this is where real DevOps hiring starts
Now I’ll teach you Kubernetes incidents (same format + real troubleshooting + commands explained)

🔥 KUBERNETES INCIDENT 1: Pod Running but Application Not Accessible

🔹 Incident

Pod status = Running ✔️
But application not accessible

🧠 What Happened

Pod is running
But:

  • App not listening OR

  • Wrong port OR

  • Service issue

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Pod Details

Bash
kubectl get pods

👉 Lists all pods and their status

2️⃣ Describe Pod

Bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

👉 Shows:

  • Events (errors, restarts)

  • Container info

3️⃣ Check Logs

Bash
kubectl logs <pod-name>

👉 Shows application logs inside pod

4️⃣ Check Port Inside Pod

Bash
kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- netstat -tulnp

👉

  • exec → run command inside pod

  • Check if app listening on correct port

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ App not listening / wrong port

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Application not exposed correctly

🔥 KUBERNETES INCIDENT 2: Pod CrashLoopBackOff

🔹 Incident

Pod status = CrashLoopBackOff

🧠 What Happened

Pod starts → crashes → restarts repeatedly

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Logs

Bash
kubectl logs <pod-name>

2️⃣ Check Previous Logs

Bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --previous

👉 Shows logs from previous crashed container

3️⃣ Describe Pod

Bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

👉 Look at:

  • Exit code

  • Restart count

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ App crash / wrong config / missing env

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Application failing on startup

🔥 KUBERNETES INCIDENT 3: Pod Pending State

🔹 Incident

Pod stuck in Pending

🧠 What Happened

Pod not scheduled to any node

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Describe Pod

Bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

👉 Look at:

  • "Insufficient CPU"

  • "No nodes available"

2️⃣ Check Nodes

Bash
kubectl get nodes

👉 Shows node status

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ No resources / node issue

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Scheduling failure

🔥 KUBERNETES INCIDENT 4: Service Not Accessible

🔹 Incident

Pod running
Service created
Still not accessible

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Service

Bash
kubectl get svc

👉 Lists services

2️⃣ Describe Service

Bash
kubectl describe svc <service-name>

👉 Check:

  • Port

  • TargetPort

  • Endpoints

3️⃣ Check Endpoints

Bash
kubectl get endpoints

👉 If empty → service not linked to pod

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Label mismatch

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Service not mapped to pod

🔥 KUBERNETES INCIDENT 5: ImagePullBackOff

🔹 Incident

Pod cannot pull image

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Describe Pod

Bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

👉 Shows image pull error

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Wrong image name / no access

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Image not available

🔥 KUBERNETES INCIDENT 6: Pod Restarting Frequently

🔹 Incident

Pod restarting again and again

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Restart Count

Bash
kubectl get pods

2️⃣ Check Logs

Bash
kubectl logs <pod-name>

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ App crashing / health check failing

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Unstable container

🔥 KUBERNETES INCIDENT 7: Node Not Ready

🔹 Incident

Node status = NotReady

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Nodes

Bash
kubectl get nodes

2️⃣ Describe Node

Bash
kubectl describe node <node-name>

👉 Shows:

  • Memory pressure

  • Disk pressure

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Node resource issue

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Node unhealthy

🔥 KUBERNETES INCIDENT 8: ConfigMap Changes Not Reflecting

🔹 Incident

Updated config
App still old

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check ConfigMap

Bash
kubectl get configmap

2️⃣ Restart Pod

Bash
kubectl delete pod <pod-name>

👉 Pod recreated with new config

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Pod not restarted

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Config not reloaded

🔥 KUBERNETES INCIDENT 9: High Pod CPU Usage

🔹 Incident

Pod consuming high CPU

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Metrics

Bash
kubectl top pod

👉 Shows CPU/memory usage

2️⃣ Check Logs

Bash
kubectl logs <pod-name>

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Heavy processing / bug

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

High resource usage

🔥 KUBERNETES INCIDENT 10: Deployment Not Updating

🔹 Incident

New version deployed
But old version still running

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Deployment

Bash
kubectl get deployment

2️⃣ Describe Deployment

Bash
kubectl describe deployment <name>

3️⃣ Force Rollout

Bash
kubectl rollout restart deployment <name>

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Rolling update issue

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Deployment not refreshed

🚀 YOU STARTED KUBERNETES 🔥

Now you are entering high-demand DevOps skill

👉 Next (VERY IMPORTANT)

Say:

👉 "next 10 k8s incidents"
👉 "k8s interview questions"
👉 "k8s hands-on lab"

💡 My advice:
👉 Continue incidents till 30 → then do lab → then interview

You’re on the right track 🚀


You said:

Next 20

ChatGPT said:

Excellent Sai Kiran 🔥 — now we go Kubernetes INCIDENTS 11–30 (real production + deeper troubleshooting mindset)

I’ll keep:

  • Same format ✅

  • Clear command explanation ✅

  • Real DevOps thinking ✅

🔥 INCIDENT 11: Pod Running but No Logs

🔹 Incident

Pod is Running
But kubectl logs shows nothing

🧠 What Happened

Application not writing logs to stdout

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Logs

Bash
kubectl logs <pod-name>

👉 If empty → app not logging

2️⃣ Enter Pod

Bash
kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- /bin/sh

👉 Opens shell inside container

3️⃣ Check Files

Bash
ls /var/log

👉 Logs might be inside file instead of stdout

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Logs not configured for stdout

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Logging misconfiguration

🔥 INCIDENT 12: Pod Stuck in Terminating

🔹 Incident

Pod not deleting

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Pod

Bash
kubectl get pod <pod-name>

2️⃣ Force Delete

Bash
kubectl delete pod <pod-name> --force --grace-period=0

👉

  • --force → immediate delete

  • --grace-period=0 → no wait

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Finalizer / stuck process

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Pod stuck during cleanup

🔥 INCIDENT 13: Service Working Internally but Not Externally

🔹 Incident

App works inside cluster
Not from browser

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Service Type

Bash
kubectl get svc

👉 Types:

  • ClusterIP → internal only

  • NodePort / LoadBalancer → external

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Wrong service type

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Service not exposed

🔥 INCIDENT 14: Pod OOMKilled

🔹 Incident

Pod terminated with OOMKilled

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Describe Pod

Bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

👉 Look for:

  • "OOMKilled"

2️⃣ Check Limits

Bash
kubectl get pod <pod-name> -o yaml

👉 Look at:

  • memory limits

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Memory limit too low

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Out of memory

🔥 INCIDENT 15: Deployment Pods Not Scaling

🔹 Incident

Increased replicas
Pods not increasing

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Deployment

Bash
kubectl get deployment

2️⃣ Describe Deployment

Bash
kubectl describe deployment <name>

👉 Check events

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Resource shortage

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Scaling failure

🔥 INCIDENT 16: Pod Cannot Connect to Another Pod

🔹 Incident

Service-to-service communication failing

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Test Inside Pod

Bash
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- ping <service-name>

2️⃣ Check Service

Bash
kubectl get svc

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ DNS / service issue

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Internal networking issue

🔥 INCIDENT 17: Image Pull Slow

🔹 Incident

Pod creation slow

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Describe Pod

Bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Large image / slow network

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Slow image pull

🔥 INCIDENT 18: Pod Running Old Version After Update

🔹 Incident

Updated image
Old version still running

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Image

Bash
kubectl get pod -o wide

2️⃣ Force Update

Bash
kubectl rollout restart deployment <name>

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Image cache

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Old image used

🔥 INCIDENT 19: Node Disk Pressure

🔹 Incident

Pods getting evicted

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Describe Node

Bash
kubectl describe node <node>

👉 Look for:

  • DiskPressure

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Disk full

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Node storage issue

🔥 INCIDENT 20: Pod Not Scheduled Due to Taints

🔹 Incident

Pod pending

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Describe Node

Bash
kubectl describe node

👉 Shows taints

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Taint mismatch

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Scheduling blocked

🔥 INCIDENT 21: Liveness Probe Failing

🔹 Incident

Pod restarting

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Describe Pod

Bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

👉 Look at:

  • Liveness probe

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Health check failing

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Probe failure

🔥 INCIDENT 22: Readiness Probe Failing

🔹 Incident

Pod running but no traffic

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Pod

Bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Not ready

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Pod not ready

🔥 INCIDENT 23: Secret Not Loaded

🔹 Incident

App cannot access credentials

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Secrets

Bash
kubectl get secrets

2️⃣ Describe Pod

Bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Secret not mounted

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Secret missing

🔥 INCIDENT 24: PVC Not Bound

🔹 Incident

Pod stuck pending

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check PVC

Bash
kubectl get pvc

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Storage issue

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Volume not available

🔥 INCIDENT 25: High Pod Memory Usage

🔹 Incident

Pod consuming memory

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Usage

Bash
kubectl top pod

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Memory leak

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

High memory

🔥 INCIDENT 26: Namespace Issue

🔹 Incident

Resources not visible

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Namespace

Bash
kubectl get pods -n <namespace>

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Wrong namespace

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Namespace mismatch

🔥 INCIDENT 27: Ingress Not Working

🔹 Incident

Domain not routing

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Ingress

Bash
kubectl get ingress

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Misconfigured ingress

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Routing issue

🔥 INCIDENT 28: Pod Not Starting Due to Init Container

🔹 Incident

Pod stuck initializing

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Init Containers

Bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ Init failure

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Init container failed

🔥 INCIDENT 29: Cluster DNS Not Working

🔹 Incident

Pods cannot resolve service names

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Test DNS

Bash
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- nslookup kubernetes.default

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ CoreDNS issue

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

DNS failure

🔥 INCIDENT 30: API Server Not Responding

🔹 Incident

kubectl commands not working

🧪 Troubleshooting

1️⃣ Check Cluster

Bash
kubectl cluster-info

🔍 ROOT CAUSE

❌ API server down

🧠 One-Line Root Cause

Control plane issue

🚀🔥 YOU COMPLETED 30 KUBERNETES INCIDENTS

👉 This is real DevOps / Kubernetes troubleshooting level
👉 Enough for interviews + job readiness

🎯 NEXT STEP (VERY IMPORTANT)

Say:

👉 "k8s interview questions"
👉 "k8s hands-on lab"
👉 "aws incidents"

💡 My strong suggestion:
👉 Now do hands-on lab — that’s what companies expect 🚀
